title: Thinking Biblically About Infertility and Miscarriage
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 1-12-25 - Thinking Biblically About Life 8 - Infertility and Miscarriage - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Hidden Suffering of Infertility and Miscarriage
subpoints: Statistics: 30-50% of all pregnancies in the United States end in miscarriage, most in the first 12 weeks

Miscarriages in the context of not yet having any children pierce the soul of a couple with questions: what is wrong with our bodies? how are we supposed to be fruitful if we cannot carry a baby? are we being punished?
This suffering often remains largely unknown to others — by the time the miscarriage happens, the pregnancy has often not yet been shared; couples often suffer alone

Primary resource: Jeremy Pray's work on this subject (approximately 200 pages); four steps: (1) consider their suffering, (2) cultivate a spirit of compassion, (3) point them to their Heavenly Father, (4) encourage them to walk wisely

outline.title: 1. Consider Their Suffering
subpoints: Both husband and wife suffer, though differently; the husband may begin moving forward emotionally before the wife, but brothers need to come and weep with the husband too
The wife suffers with particular intensity because it is in her body that things are happening; she may describe carrying a lifeless baby as being a walking tomb; she often feels alone
Romans 12:15 — weep with those who weep; this requires understanding why they are suffering so as to step into their suffering

Consider how miscarriage compels both spouses to think theologically — wrestling with serious questions alone, in their heads: Did God cause this? If God commands us to be fruitful, why isn't he allowing us? If God is sovereign and good, why this outcome? How can a good God think ending a baby's life is good? These are questions the Scriptures clearly answer, but not questions to be wrestled with alone
Proverbs 20:5 — counsel in the heart of a man is like deep water, but a man of understanding will draw it out; our role is to draw out what is in their hearts
Consider how the miscarriage burdens the marriage — sex can become a means of procreation driven by ovulation schedules, introducing strain; physical and emotional strain can introduce temptation

Consider how the miscarriage strains relationships with family and friends — even well-intentioned words can wound; examples of unhelpful things said: "you will probably be pregnant right away now," "at least it will be easier to lose the baby weight," "I just look at my wife and she gets pregnant"

outline.title: 2. Cultivate a Spirit of Compassion
subpoints: Prepare to listen — Proverbs 18:13: he who answers a matter before he hears it, it is folly and shame to him; Galatians 6:2: bear one another's burdens and so fulfill the law of Christ
Do not assume you know what they feel even if you have had similar experiences; when you make it about your experience, you become the center, not Christ or them
Be quick to cry and slow to correct at this point — save corrections for later; even if you notice wrong views of God, first weep with them
Consider what to say — pray that God helps you choose your words carefully; Proverbs 25:11: a word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver
Start by asking questions to draw them out — not as an interrogation under bright lights, slowly, giving time for answers; moments of silence are acceptable
Consider what not to say — avoid clichés; resist the temptation to tell your own story or someone else's story instead of understanding their suffering

outline.title: 3. Point Them to Their Heavenly Father
subpoints: Encourage them to lament — it is okay to cry, grieve, be sad, cry out to God; Mark Vroegop's book Dark Clouds, Deep Mercy on the lament Psalms; encourage working through Psalm 13, 25, 77, 86 in the midst of sorrow, not just after it
Help them fix their hope on Jesus Christ — Psalm 23 (understood in light of John 10: Jesus the Good Shepherd); Psalm 46 (God is our refuge); the four Gospels to see Jesus being the Good Shepherd

Show them that God is always at work even when he seems idle — Ephesians 1:11: in him we have obtained an inheritance, having been predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will; 2 Corinthians 4:17-18; Philippians 1:6; Romans 8:28-29 — the good that God is working (not just verse 28 in isolation, but verse 29 which defines the good as conformity to the image of Christ)
Habakkuk 3:17-19 — though the fig tree may not blossom nor fruit be on the vines... yet I will rejoice in the Lord, I will joy in the God of my salvation; work through Habakkuk with them

Resist the temptation to offer false hope — do not say "I am sure God will give you children eventually" or "this will soon be over"; Jesus will never disappoint; do not place their hope on anything but him

Help them understand God's view of idolatrous desires — if God is desired only as the means to have children, the children become the functional god; Psalm 16:5, 11; Psalm 42:1-2; Psalm 62:1-2; Psalm 73:25-26 — helping them long for God as God, not as a means to an end
Pray for them — do not just say you will pray; pray with them; James 5: the prayer of a righteous person avails much

Gently explore the reasons why God ordains suffering when appropriate — Romans 5:3-4 (suffering produces endurance); James 1:2-4 (suffering perfects); 2 Corinthians 4:17; 2 Corinthians 12:7-10 (grace sufficient in weakness); Hebrews 12:6-11 (discipline as evidence of sonship)

outline.title: 4. Encourage Them to Walk Wisely
subpoints: A sense of desperation can set in — wanting a baby "at all costs"; help them think wisely about their bodies and money (good stewardship)
Exhort them to remain in fellowship; the two great commandments do not take a vacation during suffering; fellowship is how we love God and others
Equip them to exercise discernment with medical care — not every option a doctor presents is one a believer can accept
When appropriate, explore the possibility of adoption (pointing back to the series on adoption)
Encourage them to sing the Psalms — Psalms that feed the soul and give words to present their suffering to God

On the eternal state of children who die in the womb or in infancy: God promises to be a God to believers and to their children; 2 Samuel 12:23 — David says of his deceased infant son, "I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me"; a pastor can with clear conscience tell a believing parent that their child who died in infancy or in the womb is in heaven

summary: This adult Sunday School lesson in the "Thinking Biblically About Life" series addresses how to minister the Word of God to couples facing infertility and miscarriage. Drawing primarily on Jeremy Pray's work, Pastor Lyro works through four steps. First, consider their suffering: both spouses suffer, the wife with particular physical intensity (one woman described carrying a lifeless baby as being a walking tomb); the miscarriage compels theological wrestling done alone; it strains the marriage (sex can become mechanical; temptation increases); and it strains relationships with well-intentioned family and friends whose words wound. Second, cultivate a spirit of compassion: prepare to listen (Proverbs 18:13), ask questions to draw them out (Proverbs 20:5), be quick to cry and slow to correct, carefully choose words (Proverbs 25:11), and observe moments of silence. Third, point them to their Heavenly Father: encourage lament using Psalms 13, 25, 77, and 86; help them fix hope on Jesus the Good Shepherd (Psalm 23 / John 10); show from Ephesians 1:11 and Romans 8:28-29 that God is always at work; work through Habakkuk 3:17-19 as a model of rejoicing in God despite circumstances; resist offering false hope; help them recognize idolatrous desires (God as a means to children rather than an end in himself); pray with and for them; gently explore why God ordains suffering using Romans 5, James 1, 2 Corinthians 4 and 12. Fourth, encourage them to walk wisely: good stewardship of body and finances; remain in fellowship; exercise discernment with medical options; consider adoption; sing the Psalms. The lesson closes by affirming that children who die in the womb or in infancy will be with God, grounded in the covenant promise (2 Samuel 12:23).
transcript:
## Introduction

We have been going through a series that considers several different subjects and how to think biblically about them. The goal is to think about these subjects biblically but also to present a methodology of how to think biblically — to demonstrate that the Bible addresses everything that is pertinent to life. I want to train us to attempt, whenever we face a situation: what is it I am facing? What does the Bible say about it?

This morning, in our series about thinking biblically, I want us to consider the impact of infertility and miscarriage on couples.

From statistics — which are difficult to establish because many miscarriages go unreported — it seems that in the United States, 30 to 50% of all pregnancies end in miscarriage, most in the first 12 weeks. Especially miscarriages that happen in the context of not yet having any children — these pierce the soul of a couple. Questions arise: what is wrong with our bodies? How are we supposed to be fruitful and multiply if we cannot carry a baby? Does this mean we will never be able to have a baby? Are we being punished for something?

As loud and repetitive as these thoughts may be, this suffering often remains largely unknown to the rest of the people around them, because it involves deeply personal matters. And often by the time the miscarriage happens, the couple has not yet shared the pregnancy with others. So they are often suffering alone.

How can we minister the Word of God to the hearts of husbands and wives facing a miscarriage? I am going to summarize Jeremy Pray's work — a 200-page book — in about 40 minutes. He suggests four steps: (1) consider their suffering; (2) cultivate a spirit of compassion; (3) point them to their Heavenly Father; (4) encourage them to walk wisely.

## 1. Consider Their Suffering

Consider how the miscarriage causes each spouse to suffer. We tend to think only the wife is suffering, but the husband is also suffering — and each experiences it differently. The husband may start moving forward emotionally and spiritually before his wife can. But brothers need to come and weep with him as well.

The wife faces many of the effects of infertility and miscarriage with special intensity. The struggle resides within her body; her physical nearness to death in her womb can be agonizing. A woman once told me that carrying a lifeless baby in her womb was like being a walking tomb.

Romans 12:15 — we are to weep with those who weep. This is not just seeing someone cry and starting to cry ourselves. It means understanding why they are suffering, so as to step into that suffering as if it were our own.

Consider also how the miscarriage compels both spouses to think theologically. We all think theologically — that is how God designed us. In the midst of suffering, couples are wrestling with: did God cause this? If God commands us to be fruitful and multiply, why is he not allowing us to do it? If God is sovereign and good, why is he choosing this outcome for us? How can a good God think that ending an unborn baby's life is a good idea? Why should God not want a child to be born into a Christian family? Is God punishing me?

These are all good questions that deserve answers. But they are questions the couple should not be wrestling with alone. Proverbs 20:5 — counsel in the heart of man is like deep water, but a man of understanding will draw it out. Our role is to draw them out, to help them work through these questions.

Consider also how the miscarriage burdens the marriage. Sexual intercourse can become a burden — what was once an expression of unity can slowly become a means of procreation, timed according to ovulation cycles. Sex may now include a strain of deep sorrow. These difficulties may lead couples to refrain from sex, introducing the temptation to find sexual fulfillment elsewhere.

And consider how the miscarriage challenges relationships with family and friends. Even well-intentioned words can inflict pain. Things like: "You will probably be pregnant right away now that you have had a miscarriage" — there is no hope in that, and it raises the fact that you lost a baby. "At least it will be easier to lose the baby weight." A husband saying to comfort another husband: "You are lucky; I just look at my wife and she gets pregnant." Or trying to comfort someone by saying, "Kids are exhausting — I was up all night with my sick kid." The family experiencing the miscarriage is thinking: I would give anything to spend a night up with my sick kid.

They may start avoiding friends and family because being alone is better than facing these kinds of statements.

## 2. Cultivate a Spirit of Compassion

Prepare to listen. Proverbs 18:13 — he who answers a matter before he hears it is folly and shame to him. Galatians 6:2 — bear one another's burdens and so fulfill the law of Christ.

Jesus sympathizes with our weaknesses (Hebrews 4:15). Think about how he would interact with them and interact with them in that way.

Do not assume you know what they feel even if you have had similar experiences. The worst thing you can do is start sharing your own experiences before understanding theirs — then you become the center, not Jesus and not them.

Be quick to cry and slow to correct. Even if you notice wrong views of God, let us cry with them first. Take time to suffer with them before moving on to corrections. Save your instruction for a later time.

Consider what to say. Pray that God will help you choose your words carefully. Proverbs 25:11 — a word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver.

Start by asking questions to draw them out. This is not an interrogation — slow it down, give plenty of time for answers. Moments of silence are okay. Our American culture is against silence, but moments of silence are acceptable. You do not have to fill them.

Consider what not to say. Avoid clichés. Resist the temptation to tell your story or someone else's story instead of understanding theirs. Proverbs 10:19 — in a multitude of words sin is not lacking, but he who restrains his lips is wise.

## 3. Point Them to Their Heavenly Father

Encourage them to lament. It is okay to cry. It is okay to grieve. It is okay to be sad. It is okay to cry out to the Lord. A great resource is Mark Vroegop's *Dark Clouds, Deep Mercy*. Encourage them to read and pray the individual songs of lament in the Psalter. Work with them through Psalm 13 — it keeps asking, "How long, O Lord, how long?" Work through Psalm 25, Psalm 77, Psalm 86. These are lament songs that will help them express their struggles.

As they do this, it will help them come to a place where they may be able to sing with David in Psalm 13:5: *But I have trusted in your mercy; my heart shall rejoice in your salvation.* This happens not after the sorrow, but in the midst of it.

Help them fix their hope on Jesus Christ. Spend time in Psalm 23 — always understood in terms of John 10, where Jesus says he is the Good Shepherd. See how Jesus will carry and protect and care for them. Psalm 46 — God is our refuge and strength; Psalm 46:10, "Be still and know that I am God." Immerse them in the four Gospels to see Jesus being the Good Shepherd.

Show them that God is always at work even when he seems idle. Ephesians 1:11 — in him we have obtained an inheritance, having been predestined according to the purpose of him who works — present tense — all things according to the counsel of his will. That includes infertility. That includes miscarriage. 2 Corinthians 4:17-18 — our light and momentary affliction is working for us a far more exceedingly eternal weight of glory.

When you use Romans 8:28, never do it by itself — because verse 28 cannot be understood without verse 29. Verse 29 tells us what the good is: *For whom he foreknew, he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son.* The good is conformity to Christ. That is what God is working even through infertility, even through a miscarriage.

Habakkuk is a great place to take them. Habakkuk cried out to God about what seemed to be unjust suffering. But then at the end of the book he is able to say: *Though the fig tree may not blossom, nor fruit be on the vines... yet I will rejoice in the Lord, I will joy in the God of my salvation.* Work through Habakkuk with those who are suffering.

Resist the temptation to offer false hope. Do not say things like "I am sure God will give you children eventually" — because you do not know that; there is no such promise in the Bible. Or "this will soon be over" — you do not know that. Placing one's hope on anything but Jesus will only lead to disappointment. Jesus will never disappoint.

Help them understand God's view of idolatrous desires. If they desire God only because he alone can give them children, the children have become the functional god. God is no longer the end but the means to an end. Help them to long for God as God. Psalm 16:5, 11; Psalm 42:1-2: *As the deer pants for the water brooks, so pants my soul for you, O God.* Psalm 62:1-2. Psalm 73:25-26: *Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is none upon earth that I desire besides you.*

Pray for them. Do not just say you will pray. Pray with them. James says the prayer of a righteous person avails much — and the reason James refers to Elijah is not because Elijah was special but because he was not: he was a man just like us.

When it seems appropriate, gently explore the reasons why God ordains suffering. Romans 5:3-4 — suffering produces endurance, endurance character, character hope. James 1:2-4 — trials produce patience; patience brings you to completion. 2 Corinthians 4:17-18 — this light and momentary affliction is producing for us an eternal weight of glory. 2 Corinthians 12:7-10 — God's grace is sufficient; his power is made perfect in weakness. Hebrews 12:6-11 — God disciplines those he loves as a sign of genuine sonship.

## 4. Encourage Them to Walk Wisely

A sense of desperation can set in, and the idea of having a baby at all costs can take hold. Help them think wisely about their bodies and their money. Good stewardship matters.

Exhort them to remain in fellowship. The two great commandments — love God and love others — do not take a vacation during suffering. Fellowship is how those commandments are practiced.

Equip them to exercise discernment with medical care. There are options a doctor may present that a Christian cannot in good conscience accept. Just because a doctor says so does not mean you should do it.

When the time is right, explore the possibility of adoption — remembering what we covered earlier in the series: adoption is not a second-class parenthood; adopted children are our children in every sense.

Encourage them to sing the Psalms — Psalms that feed their souls and give them words to present to God their suffering.

Finally: what about the eternal state of children who die in the womb or in infancy? I can with a clear conscience tell a believer who lost a child in infancy or in the womb that that child is in heaven. God promises to be a God to believers and to their children. 2 Samuel 12:23 — when David's infant son died, he said: "I will go to him, but he will not return to me." He found comfort in the thought of going to be with that child in a conscious way. That illustrates the doctrine that God is a God to us and to our children.

Next week we will start a five-part series on deconstruction — the popular phenomenon of abandoning the Christian faith. There is nothing new under the sun; Ecclesiastes says that. It is just a new packaging of what has been going on for centuries. And we will have a guest speaker, Dr. Kevin Backus, in the morning service.
